Now, I established the diaphragmatic service around five years ago, and I started joint operating with a thoracic surgeon.
We have a thoracic anesthetist who will generally always intubate the patient with a double human tube so that we can take one of the lungs down if we had to.

So on initial laparoscopy, we found that a lot of the abdominal contents were in the left hemithorax, but more importantly, the entire transverse colon, as you can see, was completely plastered into the left side.

so we go with the general uh principles of repairing any hernia that we have to go outside the sac and define the sac somehow that is the first thing that structure that is now coming into view and you will uh you'll be surprised when you see it and this is the pleura that we are pushing back whilst we are dissecting and trying to reduce this hernia on the left hand side
Now, the structure that you can see on the left is the spleen, and that was the kidney that was up in the chest.
So this was a very interesting case in which the left kidney was anterior to the left spleen because of the diaphragmatic hernia.
It seems that the posterior components had migrated.
So general principles of any hernia surgery, you dissect all around the sac, and your aim is to reduce the hernia completely along with the sac.

you can use sometimes a triangular liver retractor as i've shown you in this case to keep the contents down and then push a mess inside now the mesh is usually a composite mesh and the rough side i would usually like keeping it towards the thorax thoracic side while the smooth side is towards the abdominal side the advantage of that is you do not have any abdominal contents that adhere to this mesh
But at the same time, the other big advantage is that once the lung starts getting adherent to the mesh, there is a very limited chance of having a herniation again.
One of the biggest problems of diaphragmatic hernia repairs is the recurrence rates.
So once we have done an inner crown, we went in thoracoscopically on the left-hand side, and we started putting an outside crown of stitches in order to secure the mesh.
Here you can be much braver and go very close to thoracic contents and put deep stitches in in order to secure the mesh again.
So this is a double crown technique of fixing the mesh from the chest side.
You can also utilize this technique to fish the mesh posteriorly.
Now, one of the biggest problems in some of the diaphragmatic hernia repairs is recurrence of the hernias posteriorly where the fixation is inadequate.

in those cases what we can do is we can do pericostal stitches so we would make an incision on top of the rib and pass a suture across the rib fixing the mesh onto the rib and that ensures that the posterior fixation is adequate here you can see the fixation is right close to the to the left side of the heart but you will just see the pericostal stitches as well now vats

So you have to be quite patient in the chest to make sure.
